content:
{
  "title": "Prompting for Self-Consistent Uncertainty Estimation in Large Language Models",
  "problem": "Current LLMs often produce overconfident or poorly calibrated outputs, lacking reliable quantification of their own uncertainty directly from prompts. Existing prompting strategies rarely elicit calibrated confidence estimates without costly ensembling or fine-tuning.",
  "motivation": "Applications in healthcare, law, and scientific domains require reliable uncertainty estimates for responsible AI deployment. As LLMs are increasingly used for high-stakes decision support, improved methods to quantify and calibrate model confidence within the prompt-response loop are critical for user trust and downstream system safety.",
  "method": "We propose a novel prompting method called Self-Consistent Uncertainty Probing (SCUP). SCUP augments user queries with carefully crafted meta-prompts that ask the LLM to answer the original question multiple times with varied paraphrasing, then explicitly reflect on the consistency of its outputs. The prompt concludes by asking the model to report a calibrated confidence score, conditioned on the observed diversity in its own generated responses. This method leverages the LLM's internal uncertainty as revealed by its own variability, without requiring sampling-based ensembling or external calibration models.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate SCUP on factual question answering (TriviaQA, Natural Questions), commonsense reasoning (CommonsenseQA), and open-domain dialogue datasets. Baselines will include standard prompting and chain-of-thought with explicit confidence queries. Metrics will include calibration error (ECE), correlation between self-reported and empirical accuracy, and downstream decision quality in risk-sensitive tasks."
}
